figures.caption: Figure 6: Frequency distribution of congestion cluster size for networks of different sizes. Distribution P (S) of the spatiotemporal cluster size S plotted against the scaled variable S/ √ N . Here, N denotes the total number of intersections in the network. Results are shown for N = 9, 64, and 169. Straight lines indicate linear fits in the log–log plane, performed over the range where the number of observed clusters remains statistically significant.

Figure 1: Schematic illustration of congestion clusters. Congested regions on the network (red) at times t1, . . . , t5 are shown, indicating that a traffic jam at a given time (e.g., t3) forms a cluster as a set connected in space and time.

Figure 5: Examples of spatiotemporal structures of extracted congestion clusters. Links whose density exceeds the threshold ρthres = 0.9 are classified as congested, and clusters are defined based on spatial and temporal connectivity. The lattice at t = 0 is shown in blue, and the horizontal axis represents time t. Panels (a)-(f) display clusters ordered by size, corresponding to the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 5th, 10th, and 20th largest clusters, respectively.

Figure 4: Visualization of density distributions on a lattice network. (a) Overview of the lattice network used in the simulation. Panels (b), (c), and (d) show the density ρ on each link at times t = 0, t = 5, and t = 50, respectively, illustrating the generation, propagation, and dissipation of high-density regions (the color bar indicates density).
